entries.form_section: OA just l presumably always prefixed to the following word. Sam. occasionally the same. Early Egyptian OfA alternating lh and lˀ but elsewhere almost always with final aleph in the literary tradition and even so in Gal. and Sam. where other final long /a/ vowels are regularly rendered with heh.
entries.end_notes:
Note especially that Syriac ܠܐ ܗܘܐ "not" does not have a reference to past time.
Given that in the earliest texts simple "not" is prefixed to its verb, it is probable that the later common spelling with final aleph reflects the interjectional usage with a clear final glottal as often heard in Arabic. One might then distinguish lexically between original lā_ "not" and lāˀ" "no!" in Northwest Semitic.
The Syriac ܠܐ ܗܘܐ is replaced by ܠܘ in later texts, for which see s.v. lˀw, lw adv.

entries.form_section: JLAtg always with long /ā/, but in the tradition of the Genizah PTA texts with short /a/ when standing alone, (i.e. in a closed short syllable in construct state), /ā/ with pronominal suffixes. This latter tradition is followed by some LJLA pointed manuscripts. See the similar pattern with yt prep.
entries.end_notes:
This preposition otherwise occurs with non-personal objects only quite late, thus making the ImpAr reconstructed form dubious.
With verbs of speech in translation and later Syriac, almost certainly calqued from the Greek, as argued by Butts, Change p. 144ff.

See also s.v. mdlyt.

All dialects would seem to allow lyt before independent personal pronouns, but the combined forms are somewhat more limited in distribution to the more post-literary strata.

This particle alternates with lˀmr in OfA, thus showing that it is a colloquial shortening of the latter. See bibliography in DNWSI.
Some Syriac writers/translators, in quoting earlier writers, use the particle only in meaning 2 while citing direct citations without it, as in the cited example. The point being that the particle marks a different authorial point of view.
In poetry, the particle, normally "enclitic" after the first sense unit, can come elsewhere in the quote, when metrical considerations require it to be so, even at the very end of an extended quotation.

meanings.gloss: enclitic particle marking direct speech or part of a quotation
